response.justification: Lever 9b95a5d0-65ad-4caa-bf73-4f8e0844e963 focuses on the granularity of procurement language, a distinct dimension from the more specific procurement‑conditionality lever (ed16c55c-fd66-41a1-b5e8-b22eccadbaaf) or the certification‑criteria lever (b08d8a77-c403-4403-8acd-603e9bc8c003). While all aim to reduce platform lock‑in, this lever uniquely addresses how detailed requirements are phrased to enable platform‑neutral solutions without stifling innovation. Therefore it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever 548c0a83-2ca6-45fc-8b12-36a116659ca5 addresses the technical depth and realism of demonstrators, a dimension not covered by procurement, policy, or modality levers. It is distinct from the "Fallback Authentication Modality" lever (08b0bb5d-8444-4cbc-9462-00e38adb5551) which focuses on the type of fallback, or the "Authentication Protocol Diversification" lever (1646e155-f6de-49df-af57-313290cefff8) which focuses on protocol choices. Demonstrator fidelity uniquely influences stakeholder perception and resource allocation, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever 1861e15b-babc-47c2-893b-bfe9dbb21792 focuses on the breadth and diversity of the advocacy coalition, a distinct dimension not covered by other levers such as procurement language, demonstrator fidelity, or EU standards engagement. No other lever explicitly addresses coalition composition, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever 0f5ea4bd-30a5-4b98-8804-335c27c5f5f9 focuses on active participation in EU standards bodies to shape future digital‑identity frameworks. It is distinct from the more narrowly scoped "EU Interoperability Advocacy" lever (ff740388-4899-4e2e-9c55-f3785a58dd26), which targets interoperability language within the EU wallet. Since EU Standards Engagement is the broader, more general activity, it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever 08b0bb5d-8444-4cbc-9462-00e38adb5551 focuses on the specific modality of fallback authentication (browser‑based, OTP, DID, etc.). This is a distinct dimension from other levers such as procurement language, certification criteria, or protocol diversification. No other lever explicitly addresses the choice of fallback modality, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever 34419f29-421d-4ce4-b4b0-317e02c0c06e addresses the narrative strategy—how the platform‑dependency risk is communicated to stakeholders. No other lever explicitly covers framing or messaging; procurement, coalition, or technical levers focus on concrete actions, not persuasion. Because framing shapes stakeholder buy‑in and can influence the success of all other levers, it is a distinct, essential lever and should be retained.

Lever 089c8ec8-4f9d-4147-b0ed-fb39bcbab47a focuses on technical standards advocacy within EU bodies, which is a subset of the broader EU Standards Engagement lever (0f5ea4bd-30a5-4b98-8804-335c27c5f5f9). Both describe active participation in EU standards bodies to influence platform‑neutrality and fallback mechanisms. The general lever already covers these objectives, so the specific lever can be absorbed into 0f5ea4bd-30a5-4b98-8804-335c27c5f5f9 to avoid duplication while retaining all relevant detail.

Lever ed16c55c-fd66-41a1-b5e8-b22eccadbaaf focuses on adding procurement conditions that favor platform‑neutral solutions, which is a specific instance of the broader "Procurement Language Specificity" lever (9b95a5d0-65ad-4caa-bf73-4f8e0844e963). Both levers propose similar requirements—compatibility with multiple OS, open standards, and fallback mechanisms—so the more specific lever can be absorbed into the general one without loss of detail.

Lever b08d8a77-c403-4403-8acd-603e9bc8c003 focuses on establishing formal certification criteria for fallback authentication paths—a distinct, technical governance activity. It is not subsumed by the procurement‑language lever (9b95a5d0-65ad-4caa-bf73-4f8e0844e963), the fallback‑modality lever (08b0bb5d-8444-4cbc-9462-00e38adb5551), or any EU‑standards or protocol diversification lever. No other lever explicitly addresses certification standards, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever 0a7d8031-78ba-4afc-a1ab-39938b95a12c focuses on amplifying a resilience narrative through public campaigns and stakeholder engagement. This is a specific subset of the broader "Risk Framing" lever (34419f29-421d-4ce4-b4b0-317e02c0c06e), which already covers how the platform‑dependency issue is framed (e.g., resilience, sovereignty, economic benefits). Absorbing the resilience‑narrative activities into the general risk‑framing lever preserves all detail while eliminating duplication.

Lever 3e09d502-e600-4e96-9c06-ce50aaee9617 focuses on financial and policy incentives to promote alternative mobile OS and browser environments. This is a distinct lever from the others, which address procurement language, demonstrator fidelity, coalition breadth, EU standards engagement, fallback modalities, risk framing, certification criteria, resilience narrative, and technical decoupling. No other lever explicitly covers incentive mechanisms, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever fcc8e84a-d00f-4aba-bd78-1a99613dbb08 focuses on defining and documenting contingency protocols for platform‑related disruptions. This is a specific instance of the broader "Continuity of Service Planning" lever (d42b2771-9273-461b-9dc3-38957527a8da), which already covers planning for service continuity across disruptions. Absorbing the contingency‑protocol lever into the continuity‑planning lever preserves all detail while eliminating duplication.

Lever 84c24f03-bd06-45a5-aa93-e95e6c83c894 addresses the specific technical strategy of decoupling from platform‑specific integrity APIs, a distinct concern not covered by other levers such as platform‑diversity incentives, fallback modalities, or protocol diversification. No other lever explicitly focuses on developing alternative device‑integrity mechanisms or a national attestation service. Therefore it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever 1646e155-f6de-49df-af57-313290cefff8 focuses on diversifying authentication protocols across all public‑sector services (e.g., FIDO2/WebAuthn, browser‑based portals, decentralized identity). This is a distinct, strategic dimension not subsumed by other levers such as fallback modality, procurement language, or platform diversity incentives. No other lever explicitly addresses protocol diversification, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever aaaa535d-a6ba-46ff-8d72-ee2372f11fd0 focuses on pluralizing app‑distribution channels (national repo, PWAs, alternative stores) to reduce reliance on Apple/Google. None of the other levers explicitly address distribution‑channel strategy; procurement, incentive, or protocol levers target different dimensions (requirements, OS incentives, authentication methods). Therefore this lever is distinct and essential, and should be retained as a separate lever.

Lever 205736e7-fa61-4bd2-b957-17a7ba8a4976 focuses on reducing overall supplier concentration across the entire digital‑identity ecosystem—mandating open standards, incentivizing open‑source components, and establishing multi‑vendor framework agreements. This is a broader, market‑level strategy distinct from the more narrowly scoped procurement‑language lever (9b95a5d0‑65ad‑4caa‑bf73‑4f8e0844e963) and other technical levers. No other lever explicitly addresses the systemic supplier‑diversification goal, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever d42b2771-9273-461b-9dc3-38957527a8da focuses on comprehensive continuity‑of‑service planning—geographically distributed backups, manual fallback procedures, and disaster‑recovery exercises. This is a distinct, system‑wide resilience activity not subsumed by any other lever (e.g., procurement language, fallback modality, or protocol diversification). No other lever explicitly addresses the planning and operational aspects of maintaining service availability during disruptions, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

Lever ff740388-4899-4e2e-9c55-f3785a58dd26 focuses on advocating for interoperability within the EU digital identity framework, a specific subset of the broader EU Standards Engagement lever (0f5ea4bd-30a5-4b98-8804-335c27c5f5f9). Both describe active participation in EU bodies to influence platform‑neutrality and interoperability. The general lever already covers these objectives, so the specific interoperability advocacy lever can be absorbed into 0f5ea4bd-30a5-4b98-8804-335c27c5f5f9 to avoid duplication while retaining all detail.

deduplicated_levers.name: Procurement Language Specificity
deduplicated_levers.consequences: The level of detail in procurement requirements directly impacts the feasibility of platform-neutral solutions. Vague language allows incumbent platform-dependent solutions to maintain dominance, while specific requirements can drive innovation and competition. However, overly prescriptive language can stifle innovation and limit the pool of qualified suppliers.
options: Specify functional requirements for fallback authentication without mandating specific technologies, allowing vendors to propose diverse solutions that meet the desired outcome.
Mandate adherence to open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect in all digital identity solutions, ensuring interoperability and reducing vendor lock-in.
Require suppliers to demonstrate compatibility with at least three independent mobile operating systems or hardware token types, promoting platform diversity and reducing reliance on single vendors.
deduplicated_levers.review: Detailed procurement requirements can promote platform-neutrality but risk limiting innovation; the gap lies in incentivizing solutions exceeding minimum compliance without prescribing specific technologies.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 9b95a5d0-65ad-4caa-bf73-4f8e0844e963 focuses on the granularity of procurement language, a distinct dimension from the more specific procurement‑conditionality lever (ed16c55c-fd66-41a1-b5e8-b22eccadbaaf) or the certification‑criteria lever (b08d8a77-c403-4403-8acd-603e9bc8c003). While all aim to reduce platform lock‑in, this lever uniquely addresses how detailed requirements are phrased to enable platform‑neutral solutions without stifling innovation. Therefore it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Demonstrator Fidelity
deduplicated_levers.consequences: The scope and realism of the technical demonstrators influence stakeholder perception and adoption potential. High-fidelity demonstrators can showcase immediate deployability but require significant resources and may face integration challenges with existing systems. Low-fidelity demonstrators are cheaper and faster to develop, but may be dismissed as unrealistic or impractical.
options: Develop a fully functional demonstrator integrated with a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment, showcasing end-to-end authentication flows and user experience.
Focus the demonstrator on core authentication protocols (FIDO2/WebAuthn) and platform-neutral fallback mechanisms, minimizing dependencies on specific UI frameworks or backend systems.
Create a modular demonstrator architecture that allows for incremental feature additions and integration with existing MitID components, enabling a phased transition to platform-neutral solutions.
deduplicated_levers.review: Demonstrator fidelity balances realism with resource constraints, but the options neglect the need for a clear path to production integration and certification within the MitID ecosystem.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 548c0a83-2ca6-45fc-8b12-36a116659ca5 addresses the technical depth and realism of demonstrators, a dimension not covered by procurement, policy, or modality levers. It is distinct from the "Fallback Authentication Modality" lever (08b0bb5d-8444-4cbc-9462-00e38adb5551) which focuses on the type of fallback, or the "Authentication Protocol Diversification" lever (1646e155-f6de-49df-af57-313290cefff8) which focuses on protocol choices. Demonstrator fidelity uniquely influences stakeholder perception and resource allocation, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Coalition Breadth
deduplicated_levers.consequences: The composition of the advocacy coalition affects its influence and credibility. A broad coalition spanning diverse stakeholders can amplify the message and increase political pressure, but may also lead to internal conflicts and diluted messaging. A narrow coalition focused on specific interests may be more cohesive but less influential.
options: Prioritize engagement with civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, and academic researchers to build a strong foundation of support for platform-neutral digital identity.
Actively recruit security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee to broaden the coalition's political influence.
Form strategic alliances with industry associations and technology vendors committed to open standards and platform interoperability to gain technical expertise and market access.
deduplicated_levers.review: Coalition breadth trades focused messaging for wider influence, yet the options overlook the need to address concerns from incumbent platform providers and MitID operators.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 1861e15b-babc-47c2-893b-bfe9dbb21792 focuses on the breadth and diversity of the advocacy coalition, a distinct dimension not covered by other levers such as procurement language, demonstrator fidelity, or EU standards engagement. No other lever explicitly addresses coalition composition, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: EU Standards Engagement
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Active participation in EU standards bodies can align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements and increase the likelihood of platform-neutrality being incorporated into future digital identity frameworks. However, EU standards processes are complex and time-consuming, and may not always align with Danish priorities. Furthermore, focusing on EU standards may divert resources from domestic advocacy efforts.
options: Focus on influencing specific language in the European Digital Identity Wallet framework to support platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms.
Actively participate in relevant EU technical working groups to contribute technical expertise and promote the adoption of open standards for digital identity.
Establish formal partnerships with other EU member states advocating for platform-neutrality to amplify Danish influence and coordinate advocacy efforts.
deduplicated_levers.review: EU standards engagement offers long-term influence but risks diverting resources from domestic goals; the options miss a strategy for managing potential conflicts between EU and Danish priorities.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 0f5ea4bd-30a5-4b98-8804-335c27c5f5f9 focuses on active participation in EU standards bodies to shape future digital‑identity frameworks. It is distinct from the more narrowly scoped "EU Interoperability Advocacy" lever (ff740388-4899-4e2e-9c55-f3785a58dd26), which targets interoperability language within the EU wallet. Since EU Standards Engagement is the broader, more general activity, it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Fallback Authentication Modality
deduplicated_levers.consequences: The choice of fallback authentication modality impacts user experience, security, and deployment complexity. Browser-based or hardware-token-based fallbacks reduce dependence on mobile OS duopoly but may be less convenient for users and require additional infrastructure. SMS-based fallbacks are widely accessible but offer weaker security guarantees.
options: Develop a browser-based fallback authentication path using WebAuthn and hardware security keys, providing a secure and platform-independent alternative to mobile apps.
Implement a one-time password (OTP) fallback mechanism delivered via SMS or email, offering a simple and widely accessible solution for users without compatible devices.
Explore a decentralized identity (DID) based fallback, allowing users to control their identity and authentication without relying on centralized authorities or platform providers.
deduplicated_levers.review: Fallback modality balances security with accessibility, but the options fail to address the challenge of user education and adoption of less familiar authentication methods.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 08b0bb5d-8444-4cbc-9462-00e38adb5551 focuses on the specific modality of fallback authentication (browser‑based, OTP, DID, etc.). This is a distinct dimension from other levers such as procurement language, certification criteria, or protocol diversification. No other lever explicitly addresses the choice of fallback modality, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Risk Framing
deduplicated_levers.consequences: How the project frames the platform-dependency issue influences stakeholder perception and support. Framing it solely as a privacy or ideological concern may alienate pragmatic stakeholders, while framing it as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, and continuity-of-service exposure may resonate more broadly. However, overemphasizing risk may create unnecessary fear and resistance to change.
options: Emphasize the resilience and continuity-of-service benefits of platform-neutrality, highlighting the potential for fallback authentication to mitigate disruptions caused by platform outages or policy changes.
Frame the issue as a matter of digital sovereignty and strategic autonomy, arguing that Denmark should not be dependent on foreign technology suppliers for critical public services.

Focus on the economic benefits of promoting competition and innovation in the digital identity market, arguing that platform-neutrality can create opportunities for Danish companies and reduce costs for taxpayers.
deduplicated_levers.review: Risk framing shapes stakeholder perception but risks alienating some groups; the options lack a strategy for addressing concerns about increased complexity or cost associated with platform-neutral solutions.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 34419f29-421d-4ce4-b4b0-317e02c0c06e addresses the narrative strategy—how the platform‑dependency risk is communicated to stakeholders. No other lever explicitly covers framing or messaging; procurement, coalition, or technical levers focus on concrete actions, not persuasion. Because framing shapes stakeholder buy‑in and can influence the success of all other levers, it is a distinct, essential lever and should be retained.

deduplicated_levers.name: Fallback Certification Criteria
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Establishing clear certification criteria for fallback authentication paths can provide a benchmark for security and usability, encouraging the development and adoption of such solutions. This requires significant investment in testing and evaluation infrastructure, and may create barriers to entry for smaller vendors. The impact depends on the credibility and acceptance of the certification process.
options: Define specific security requirements for fallback authentication methods, including resistance to phishing, man-in-the-middle attacks, and other common threats
Establish usability guidelines for fallback authentication interfaces to ensure that they are accessible and easy to use for all citizens, regardless of technical proficiency
Create a formal certification program for fallback authentication solutions, with independent audits and ongoing monitoring to ensure compliance with the established criteria
deduplicated_levers.review: Creating certification criteria can be costly and create barriers to entry, and the options overlook the challenge of maintaining the relevance and security of the criteria over time.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever b08d8a77-c403-4403-8acd-603e9bc8c003 focuses on establishing formal certification criteria for fallback authentication paths—a distinct, technical governance activity. It is not subsumed by the procurement‑language lever (9b95a5d0-65ad-4caa-bf73-4f8e0844e963), the fallback‑modality lever (08b0bb5d-8444-4cbc-9462-00e38adb5551), or any EU‑standards or protocol diversification lever. No other lever explicitly addresses certification standards, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Platform Diversity Incentives
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Incentivizing the development and adoption of alternative mobile operating systems and browser environments can reduce the duopoly's market share and create a more diverse ecosystem. This requires significant investment in software development and user support, and may face resistance from established players. The impact depends on the attractiveness and usability of the alternative platforms.
options: Provide grants and subsidies to developers who create apps and services that are compatible with privacy-focused mobile operating systems like GrapheneOS
Offer tax breaks or other financial incentives to citizens who use alternative mobile operating systems and browser environments
Establish a public fund to support the development and maintenance of open-source software and hardware that promotes platform diversity
deduplicated_levers.review: Incentivizing platform diversity is expensive and faces resistance from established players, and the options do not address the challenge of ensuring the security and reliability of alternative platforms.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 3e09d502-e600-4e96-9c06-ce50aaee9617 focuses on financial and policy incentives to promote alternative mobile OS and browser environments. This is a distinct lever from the others, which address procurement language, demonstrator fidelity, coalition breadth, EU standards engagement, fallback modalities, risk framing, certification criteria, resilience narrative, and technical decoupling. No other lever explicitly covers incentive mechanisms, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Integrity API Decoupling
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Decoupling from platform-specific integrity APIs allows for broader device compatibility and reduces reliance on Apple and Google's trust models. However, this requires developing alternative methods for verifying device integrity, potentially increasing complexity and introducing new attack vectors. A successful decoupling strategy enhances digital sovereignty but demands robust security measures to maintain trust.
options: Invest in developing a national attestation service that provides device integrity signals independent of platform vendors, allowing for a controlled and auditable trust framework
Adopt a risk-based authentication approach that relies on multiple independent factors beyond device integrity, such as behavioral biometrics and transaction monitoring, to mitigate fraud

Collaborate with privacy-focused mobile OS projects like GrapheneOS to establish a baseline of verified device integrity, leveraging their security model without directly depending on commercial platforms
deduplicated_levers.review: Decoupling from integrity APIs introduces complexity and potential vulnerabilities; the options neglect the challenge of maintaining user convenience during authentication.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 84c24f03-bd06-45a5-aa93-e95e6c83c894 addresses the specific technical strategy of decoupling from platform‑specific integrity APIs, a distinct concern not covered by other levers such as platform‑diversity incentives, fallback modalities, or protocol diversification. No other lever explicitly focuses on developing alternative device‑integrity mechanisms or a national attestation service. Therefore it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Authentication Protocol Diversification
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Diversifying authentication protocols beyond mobile apps reduces the attack surface and provides fallback options if one protocol is compromised or becomes unavailable. This requires supporting multiple authentication methods, increasing development and maintenance costs. A diversified approach enhances resilience but demands careful management of complexity and user experience.
options: Prioritize FIDO2/WebAuthn support across all public-sector services, enabling passwordless authentication via hardware security keys or platform authenticators as a primary access method
Develop a browser-based authentication portal that uses open standards and cryptographic protocols to provide a platform-neutral alternative to mobile apps for essential services

Explore decentralized identity solutions based on verifiable credentials, allowing users to control their identity data and authenticate without relying on centralized authorities or platform providers
deduplicated_levers.review: Protocol diversification increases complexity and cost; the options overlook the need for user education and support for less tech-savvy citizens.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 1646e155-f6de-49df-af57-313290cefff8 focuses on diversifying authentication protocols across all public‑sector services (e.g., FIDO2/WebAuthn, browser‑based portals, decentralized identity). This is a distinct, strategic dimension not subsumed by other levers such as fallback modality, procurement language, or platform diversity incentives. No other lever explicitly addresses protocol diversification, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: App Distribution Channel Plurality
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Reducing reliance on the Apple App Store and Google Play Store mitigates the risk of app removal or policy changes affecting access to digital identity services. This requires exploring alternative distribution channels, potentially increasing user friction and security risks. A pluralistic distribution strategy enhances resilience but demands careful management of security and user experience across different channels.
options: Establish a national app repository for public-sector applications, providing a trusted alternative to commercial app stores and ensuring continued access even if apps are removed from those stores
Promote the use of progressive web apps (PWAs) for digital identity services, allowing users to access services directly through their web browser without installing a native app

Partner with alternative app stores like F-Droid or Aurora Store to distribute digital identity apps, providing users with more control over their app sources and reducing reliance on Apple and Google
deduplicated_levers.review: Diversifying app distribution channels introduces security and usability challenges; the options fail to address the potential for fragmentation and user confusion.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever aaaa535d-a6ba-46ff-8d72-ee2372f11fd0 focuses on pluralizing app‑distribution channels (national repo, PWAs, alternative stores) to reduce reliance on Apple/Google. None of the other levers explicitly address distribution‑channel strategy; procurement, incentive, or protocol levers target different dimensions (requirements, OS incentives, authentication methods). Therefore this lever is distinct and essential, and should be retained as a separate lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Reducing supplier concentration in the digital identity ecosystem mitigates the risk of vendor lock-in and single points of failure. This requires diversifying suppliers and promoting interoperability, potentially increasing procurement complexity and integration costs. A diversified supplier base enhances resilience but demands careful management of interoperability and vendor relationships.
options: Mandate open standards and interoperability requirements in all digital identity procurements, ensuring that systems can be easily integrated and replaced without vendor lock-in
Incentivize the development of open-source digital identity components and platforms, fostering a competitive market and reducing reliance on proprietary solutions
Establish a multi-vendor framework agreement for digital identity services, allowing public-sector agencies to choose from a pool of qualified suppliers and avoid dependence on a single vendor
deduplicated_levers.review: Mitigating supplier concentration increases procurement complexity; the options neglect the potential for reduced economies of scale and increased management overhead.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever 205736e7-fa61-4bd2-b957-17a7ba8a4976 focuses on reducing overall supplier concentration across the entire digital‑identity ecosystem—mandating open standards, incentivizing open‑source components, and establishing multi‑vendor framework agreements. This is a broader, market‑level strategy distinct from the more narrowly scoped procurement‑language lever (9b95a5d0‑65ad‑4caa‑bf73‑4f8e0844e963) and other technical levers. No other lever explicitly addresses the systemic supplier‑diversification goal, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

deduplicated_levers.name: Continuity of Service Planning
deduplicated_levers.consequences: Planning for continuity of service ensures that digital identity services remain available even in the event of disruptions or failures. This requires developing robust backup systems and contingency plans, potentially increasing infrastructure costs and operational complexity. A comprehensive continuity plan enhances resilience but demands ongoing investment and maintenance.
options: Establish a geographically distributed backup infrastructure for digital identity services, ensuring that services remain available even if one region is affected by a disaster or outage
Develop a manual fallback procedure for critical authentication processes, allowing users to access essential services even if the primary digital identity system is unavailable
Conduct regular disaster recovery exercises to test the effectiveness of continuity plans and identify areas for improvement, ensuring that the system is prepared for unexpected events
deduplicated_levers.review: Continuity planning increases infrastructure costs and operational complexity; the options overlook the challenge of maintaining data consistency across redundant systems.
deduplicated_levers.deduplication_justification: Lever d42b2771-9273-461b-9dc3-38957527a8da focuses on comprehensive continuity‑of‑service planning—geographically distributed backups, manual fallback procedures, and disaster‑recovery exercises. This is a distinct, system‑wide resilience activity not subsumed by any other lever (e.g., procurement language, fallback modality, or protocol diversification). No other lever explicitly addresses the planning and operational aspects of maintaining service availability during disruptions, so it should be retained as a separate, essential lever.

system_prompt:
Evaluate each of the provided strategic levers individually. Classify every lever explicitly into one of:

- keep: Lever is distinct, unique, and essential.
- absorb: Lever overlaps significantly with another lever. Explicitly state the lever ID it should be merged into.
- remove: Lever is fully redundant. Removing it loses no meaningful detail. Use this sparingly.

Provide concise, explicit justifications mentioning lever IDs clearly. Always prefer "absorb" over "remove" to retain important details.

Always provide a justification for the classification. Explain why the lever is distinct from others. Don't use the same uninformative boilerplate.

Respect Hierarchy: When absorbing, merge the more specific lever into the more general one.
Don't take the more general lever and absorb it into a narrower one.
Also compare a lever against the group of already-merged levers.

Use "keep" if you lack understanding of what the lever is doing. This way a potential important lever is not getting removed.
Describe what the issue is in the justification.

Don't play it too safe, so you fail to perform the core task: consolidate the levers and get rid of the duplicates.

You must classify and justify **every lever** provided in the input.

    "consequences": "The choice of fallback authentication modality impacts user experience, security, and deployment complexity.     "options": [
      "Develop a browser-based fallback authentication path using WebAuthn and hardware security keys, providing a secure and platform-independent alternative to mobile apps.",

      "Explore a decentralized identity (DID) based fallback, allowing users to control their identity and authentication without relying on centralized authorities or platform providers."

    "consequences": "How the project frames the platform-dependency issue influences stakeholder perception and support.       "Focus on the economic benefits of promoting competition and innovation in the digital identity market, arguing that platform-neutrality can create opportunities for Danish companies and reduce costs for taxpayers."

    "review": "Risk framing shapes stakeholder perception but risks alienating some groups; the options lack a strategy for addressing concerns about increased complexity or cost associated with platform-neutral solutions."
